narrative_summary:
The BRCA2 missense change K1690N (c.5070A>C, p.Lys1690Asn) has been detected in a variety of cancer‑related sequencing efforts but has not been definitively linked to a hereditary cancer syndrome. It appears in colorectal cancer screening cohorts, breast cancer tumor panels, and a myeloproliferative neoplasm case, yet no consistent disease association has been established.
In clinical reports the variant has received conflicting interpretations. A multifactorial likelihood assessment classified K1690N as Class 1, indicating a benign effect [PMC3904950]. Conversely, tumor‑only sequencing of an advanced breast cancer patient described the alteration as a variant of uncertain significance (VUS) [PMC10558443], and a targeted exome study of myeloproliferative neoplasms listed it as a somatic mutation in a single patient without assigning pathogenicity [PMC4017260].
No functional assays addressing the impact of K1690N on BRCA2 protein activity or DNA repair have been reported in the extracted literature.
Population data show the variant was identified among Turkish colorectal cancer patients, but the study did not provide a pathogenicity classification or frequency estimate beyond its presence in the cohort [PMC7379412]. Computational prediction using the Condel meta‑score yielded a modest value of 0.536 (below typical thresholds for deleteriousness) and the authors did not draw a specific conclusion about clinical relevance [PMC4429836].
Taken together, the preponderance of evidence points toward a likely benign interpretation for BRCA2 K1690N, although the designation of uncertain significance in at least one tumor sequencing context reflects residual uncertainty. Additional segregation data, functional studies, or larger population frequency analyses would be needed to resolve this ambiguity.

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The study screened Turkish colorectal cancer patients and reported the presence of the BRCA2 K1690N (c.5070A>C) missense variant among other variants, without assigning a pathogenicity classification to it.
article_mentions.title: Spectrum of germline cancer susceptibility gene mutations in Turkish colorectal cancer patients: a single center study

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The study classified the BRCA2 K1690N (c.5070A>C) variant as Class 1, indicating it is not clinically significant (benign).',
article_mentions.title: Multifactorial Likelihood Assessment of BRCA1 and BRCA2 Missense Variants Confirms That BRCA1:c.122A>G(p.His41Arg) Is a Pathogenic Mutation

mentions.quoted_text: dTwo patients without a tBRCA1/2mut had BRCA2 variants of unknown pathogenic significance distinct from their gBRCA2mut: First patient: gBRCA2mut = 9345 G > C (P3039P) with tBRCA2variant = **5070 A > C** (**K1690N**); Second patient: gBRCA2mut = duplicate exons 15-18 with tBRCA2variant = 7052 C > T (A2351V).

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The study notes that the BRCA2 K1690N variant was identified in tumor sequencing of a patient and classified as of unknown pathogenic significance (VUS).',
article_mentions.title: Genomic analysis of advanced breast cancer tumors from talazoparib-treated gBRCA1/2mut carriers in the ABRAZO study

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The study used NGS to discover BRCA2 variants in Polish breast/ovarian cancer patients and reports K1690N in a supplementary table with a Condel score but without an explicit pathogenicity interpretation.
article_mentions.title: New recurrent BRCA1/2 mutations in Polish patients with familial breast/ovarian cancer detected by next generation sequencing

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The review discusses chordoma therapeutic strategies and enumerates several BRCA2 variants, including K1690N, but provides no interpretation of its significance.
article_mentions.title: Emerging target discovery and drug repurposing opportunities in chordoma
